topicArea: Gardens, Co-Authorship with Nature & the Argument Over the Fence

questions.passage:
The fence divides two philosophies. On one side, hedges squared to the centimeter, roses staked in ranks, a lawn with the nap of a billiard table; on the other, a deliberate tangle, grasses gone to seed, a path that must be believed to be seen, mown fresh each week through grass left standing on purpose. Each neighbor, watering in the evening, privately considers the other's garden a mistake. Neither has noticed that they are conducting a debate, or that the debate is very old, or that the medium in which they argue is one of the few that answers back.

A garden is neither wilderness nor architecture, and its interest lies exactly in the refusal of both. It is nature edited: selected, arranged, encouraged, suppressed, but never simply built, because the material has intentions of its own. Paint does not grow overnight; marble does not die of frost or seed itself in the neighbor's border. The gardener works in a medium that keeps working after she stops, which makes gardening the strangest of the arts, one in which authorship is permanently shared and the collaborator never signs the contract.

The two sides of the fence descend from named traditions, and each is a philosophy wearing plants. The formal garden, clipped, axial, geometric, proposes that nature is perfected by reason: the hedge's right angle is an argument that order is the human gift to growth. The wild garden answers that the highest art conceals itself, arranging its effects to look unarranged, and its paradox is famous among those who attempt it: the meadow that appears never to have been touched is among the most labor-intensive things a gardener can make. The artlessness is the artifice, and it must be renewed weekly or it becomes, simply, neglect.

The word itself remembers walls. Garden descends from enclosure, and the oldest garden words name a space marked off from the world, the walled park that gave paradise its name, the cloister square, the kitchen plot fenced against deer. Enclosure is not incidental but constitutive: a garden is a correction of the world, a bounded demonstration of how things might be if someone cared, and every garden quietly announces a judgment about what the unbounded world lacks, shade, order, abundance, quiet, depending on the gardener's complaint.

The argument turns public at the front yard. The lawn, that uniform green rectangle, is display turf, a show of control and of leisure enough to maintain what produces nothing, and it now has organized critics: the native-plant movement indicts it as an ecological desert, an imported aesthetic imposed on climates that cannot honestly support it, and would replace it with the region's own species and their attendant insects. Defenders answer that shared green carries social meanings, continuity, care, the visible commons of a street, that a movement dismisses at its cost. The quarrel looks horticultural and is actually constitutional: it asks what front gardens are for, display, habitat, or commons, and who gets to decide.

Gardens also school their keepers in time. A garden is never finished, only in various stages of becoming something else; the gardener plants bulbs for a spring she must wait for and oaks for a shade she will never sit in, and the practice teaches, better than most teachers, the arts of patience and of loss, since things die annually and the gardener composts the failure and continues. The instant landscape, mature trees craned over the wall, a garden purchased at full growth, is the one genuinely modern invention in the field: the attempt to buy not plants but time itself, and what it purchases looks like a garden the way a bought biography looks like a life.

At the fence, then, both neighbors are right about the one thing that matters, though neither would put it this way: a garden is an argument about how human beings should stand toward the living world, and both the clipped hedge and the seeded meadow are positions in it. What neither controls is the medium's reply. The gardener proposes; the garden disposes; the season revises; and what grows, on either side of the fence, is the negotiated draft, co-authored by a partner who never explains and is never entirely wrong.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A garden is nature edited in a medium that answers back, so every garden is a co-authored argument about how humans should stand toward the living world.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The native-plant movement has definitively exposed the lawn as an ecological desert, settling the constitutional question of what front gardens are for.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the wild garden secretly requires far more labor than the formal one, its claim to naturalness is fraudulent and the formal tradition is more honest.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gardens teach patience and loss, which is why instant landscaping has failed to find a market.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage defines the garden as nature edited, authorship permanently shared with a collaborator who never signs, reads the two styles as philosophies wearing plants, extends the argument through walls, lawns, and time, and closes at the fence: both gardens are positions in an argument about how humans should stand toward the living world, and what grows is the negotiated draft.
explanations.B: This choice awards a verdict the fifth paragraph withholds. The indictment and the defense are both stated, ecological desert against the visible commons of a street, and the quarrel is called constitutional precisely because it remains open, ending on who gets to decide, not on a decision.
explanations.C: This choice converts the third paragraph's paradox into an accusation. The meadow's hidden labor is presented as the wild garden's famous irony, the artlessness is the artifice, not as fraud; both traditions are philosophies, and the passage ranks neither.
explanations.D: This choice pairs a real theme with an invented market outcome. The temporal schooling is the sixth paragraph's subject, but instant landscaping is described as the field's one modern invention, its existence presupposed; nothing reports its commercial failure.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: argue that municipalities should explicitly permit naturalistic native plantings in residential front yards nationwide.

choices.label: B
choices.text: demonstrate that the lawn's defenders have misunderstood the ecology of imported turf grasses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: carry the garden argument into public space, staging the lawn quarrel as constitutional: display, habitat, or commons.

choices.label: D
choices.text: explain how the enclosure described in the fourth paragraph gave way to the open front yard.

explanations.A: This choice extracts a zoning position from a staged dispute. The movement's indictment and the defenders' answer are both presented with their strongest cards, and the paragraph closes on the question of who gets to decide rather than on a permit recommendation.
explanations.B: This choice hands victory to one side mid-quarrel. The defenders' case, shared green as continuity, care, the visible commons, is presented as an answer the movement dismisses at its cost, language that credits the defense rather than convicting it of ecological error.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph opens by announcing the move, the argument turns public at the front yard, and stages both sides: the lawn as display turf indicted as ecological desert, the defense of shared green's social meanings. The close names the stakes exactly: the quarrel looks horticultural and is actually constitutional, asking what front gardens are for, display, habitat, or commons, and who gets to decide.
explanations.D: This choice invents a historical transition the passage never narrates. The fourth paragraph's walls are constitutive of the garden idea, not a phase the front yard ended; no story of enclosure giving way appears anywhere.

questions.stem: The claim that the artlessness is the artifice, in the third paragraph, most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: wild gardens eventually revert to genuine self-sustaining wilderness if their keepers simply stop intervening in them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: formal gardeners secretly admire the meadow style they publicly reject.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the wild garden's untouched appearance is itself the constructed effect, produced by intensive concealed labor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: gardening manuals exaggerate the difficulty of maintaining naturalistic plantings.

explanations.A: This choice mistakes the sentence's warning for its meaning. The renewal clause, weekly or it becomes, simply, neglect, notes that abandonment dissolves the effect; the phrase itself names what the effect is while maintained: looking untouched as the made thing.
explanations.B: This choice invents a psychology across the fence. The neighbors' mutual disapproval frames the passage, but the phrase concerns the wild garden's internal paradox, not any concealed admiration between the traditions.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph sets up the paradox, the meadow that appears never to have been touched is among the most labor-intensive things a gardener can make, and the phrase compresses it: the apparent absence of art is the art, an arranged unarrangement in the tradition where the highest art conceals itself, requiring constant renewal to keep looking like nothing was done.
explanations.D: This choice relocates the labor claim to the bookshelf. The passage asserts the intensity of the work as the tradition's famous paradox, not as a publishing exaggeration; manuals appear nowhere.

questions.stem: According to the fifth paragraph, the native-plant movement holds that the lawn:

choices.label: A
choices.text: should be preserved in public parks while being retired from private yards.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is an ecological desert, an imported aesthetic imposed on unsuitable climates, better replaced by regional species.

choices.label: C
choices.text: carries deep social meanings of continuity and neighborly care that decisively outweigh its documented environmental costs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: was originally designed to display leisure but has since become genuinely democratic.

explanations.A: This choice invents a public-private compromise the movement never offers. The indictment targets the lawn as such, ecological desert, imported aesthetic, and the replacement program is regional species, with no park exemption mentioned.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph attributes the position in full: the native-plant movement indicts it as an ecological desert, an imported aesthetic imposed on climates that cannot honestly support it, and would replace it with the region's own species and their attendant insects. All three planks, the indictment, the diagnosis, the remedy, belong to the movement by name.
explanations.C: This choice hands the movement its opponents' brief. Continuity, care, and the visible commons are the defenders' answer, presented as what the movement dismisses at its cost, not as anything the movement holds.
explanations.D: This choice blends the author's framing with a democratization story no one tells. Display turf and leisure enough to maintain what produces nothing is the passage's own description of the lawn; neither side describes it as having become democratic.

questions.stem: A developer markets estate homes with grounds installed in a week: full-grown oaks craned over the wall, instant hedges, turf unrolled like carpet. Based on the sixth paragraph, the author would most likely say the developer is selling:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the formal tradition's perfected order, since crane and carpet realize geometry far faster than patient growth ever can.

choices.label: B
choices.text: an honest and useful convenience, since the passage itself grants that not every homeowner can reasonably wait decades for shade.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a defensible commons, since mature plantings green the street for neighbors immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the appearance of time rather than the practice of it, a bought garden resembling a garden as a bought biography resembles a life.

explanations.A: This choice files the crane under the wrong tradition. The formal garden is a philosophy of reasoned order grown and maintained; the sixth paragraph classes the instant landscape as the field's one modern invention, defined by purchasing time, not by its geometry.
explanations.B: This choice supplies a concession the paragraph never makes. No exemption for the impatient appears; the waiting is presented as the practice's content, bulbs for a spring one must wait for, oaks for shade one will never sit in, not as an inconvenience the market may fairly remove.
explanations.C: This choice borrows the fifth paragraph's defense for a different quarrel. The visible commons argument defends shared green against the native-plant indictment; the sixth paragraph's complaint about instant landscapes concerns time and its purchase, which street-greening speed does not answer.
explanations.D: Correct. The sixth paragraph describes the product exactly: mature trees craned over the wall, a garden purchased at full growth, the attempt to buy not plants but time itself, and delivers the verdict this choice carries, what it purchases looks like a garden the way a bought biography looks like a life. The developer sells the appearance of the practice with the practice removed, the waiting, the loss, the composted failures that make a garden a schooling.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the fourth paragraph's claim that every garden announces a judgment about what the unbounded world lacks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Surveys finding most gardens copied from neighbors and pattern books, contents chosen by convention, keepers unable to articulate any corrective view.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that the walled gardens that gave paradise its name were designed primarily for royalty.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that many gardeners, when asked, describe their plots primarily as private refuges from the mounting pressures of their working and family lives.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that some gardens are planted primarily to grow food rather than for beauty.

explanations.A: Correct. The claim reads every garden as a bounded demonstration of how things might be if someone cared, an announced judgment on the world's lacks. Gardens overwhelmingly copied by convention, contents set by pattern books and availability, with keepers holding no articulable corrective vision, would dissolve the judgment into imitation: enclosures without complaints, the universal claim contradicted at its base.
explanations.B: This choice narrows the etymology's original clientele, which the claim never depends on. Royal walled parks announcing royal judgments are still announcements; who first afforded the correction says nothing about whether gardens make one.
explanations.C: This choice restates the claim in the gardeners' own vocabulary. A refuge from daily pressures is precisely a judgment about what the unbounded world lacks, quiet, in the paragraph's own list, so the testimony supports the thesis rather than weakening it.
explanations.D: This choice offers the kitchen plot, which the paragraph already contains, fenced against deer, and a food garden announces abundance as its correction; utility is one of the complaints, not an exception to the claim.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the second paragraph's claim that gardening's authorship is permanently shared with a medium that keeps working after the gardener stops?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that professional garden designers command higher fees than most other landscape workers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surveys finding that most gardeners consult planting calendars before beginning each season.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that some famous historic gardens have been maintained essentially unchanged for several centuries by large teams of dedicated professional staff.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Studies finding gardens diverging steadily from their planting plans, experienced gardeners revising intentions in response to what the plants did unbidden.

explanations.A: This choice prices the designer's labor, which bears on the profession's market, not on the medium's agency; high fees are equally consistent with a fully obedient material and say nothing about shared authorship.
explanations.B: This choice documents preparation, which fits either account: calendars could serve a gardener who dictates outcomes or one who negotiates them, and consulting schedules reveals nothing about whether the medium answers back.
explanations.C: This choice runs against the claim's grain. Gardens held unchanged for centuries showcase successful human control, staff overwhelming the medium's initiatives; permanence achieved by dedicated teams is authorship defended, not shared.
explanations.D: Correct. The claim is that the material has intentions of its own and keeps working after the gardener stops, making authorship permanently shared. Gardens drifting steadily from their plans supplies the collaborator's unbidden contributions, and experienced gardeners revising intentions in response completes the negotiation, the proposal amended by the reply, the co-authorship documented in both directions.
